title: does latency really matter that much in bot trading?
author: EndlessKnight_154
selftext:
I’m pretty new to building trading bots and I’m starting to realize something I didn’t think about before… latency might actually matter a lot more than I expected.

Even if the strategy looks good in backtesting, I feel like small delays in execution could completely change the results in live trading, especially in fast markets like crypto or gold.

For those of you who are already running bots, how do you deal with latency? Do you focus on optimizing code, changing brokers, or using a VPS setup? I’m trying to understand what actually makes the biggest difference in real trading.

comments.author: phithanh1230
comments.body:
I don't know but the latency is not that much important in bot trading, there are so many apis provide really fast nowaday like polygon, alpaca, realmarketapi,...

Just focus on strategy, risk management

comments.author: sagesofthe4th_world
comments.body:
Not one bit. I trade Solana memes > 100K already bonded. 

My bots will hold for several days. I take a more mindful approach to trading. 

  
I realize for some setups speed is of the essence. But not for mine.

comments.author: Spartacus_Nakamoto
comments.body: It depends on what you’re trying to do. I’m swing trading spy options and I have a bot that checks in once per day. The latency could be 15 minutes and it wouldn’t matter.

comments.author: Stonks-1991
comments.body:
Everyone's chasing Al media plays at $5B+ valuations. $RVYL is doing Al + DeFi + Web3 for media at a fraction of that. 

Team includes James C. Heckman... who built Hulu's strategy and the guy who invented DeFi. The asymmetry here is wild.

comments.author: Cute-Let-4605
comments.body: I trade NQ and ES futures off the 1 min chart. It takes about 500ms for the algo to decide, but my risk is up to 25-50 points for NQ (depending on volatility) and 5-10 for ES. I do have a VPS as it’s nice to have power and network redundancy, it’s never fun being in a trade when you lose internet.

comments.author: Annual-Register-3683
comments.body:
Yeah you’re not overthinking it man, latency does matter, but it depends a lot on the kind of bot you’re running.

If it’s something super fast like scalping or news-based trades, even small delays can mess with your entries and exits. But if your bot is more swing or position-based, a few milliseconds won’t really change much.

From what I’ve seen, the biggest difference usually comes from where your bot is running. Having it close to your broker’s server and keeping it online 24/7 helps more than just tweaking code endlessly. I run mine on tradingfx VPS and it made execution more consistent, especially during fast moves.

comments.author: PracticalAlpacaTreas
comments.body: depends almost entirely on your holding period and what triggers your entry. if youre firing on a level break in crypto on 1m candles, 200ms delay can mean your fill is 30 bps worse because the move already ran. if youre rebalancing a portfolio at end of day based on signals, you could be running on a potato in a shed and it wouldnt matter. figure out your average holding time first, then the latency question answers itself
